patch.value:
At decades scale the overwhelming majority of a workspace is closed history. Today context assembly either ignores it (losing the recorded knowledge: decisions, learnings, why-nows) or pays full per-item cost to include it. There is no middle representation between 'full item' and 'absent'.

Proposal (long horizon, after pm-801d and pm-4k6b): a derived rollup layer, SDK-first.
- Cluster closed items by natural epoch boundaries (release, epic subtree, time window) into compact summary records: scope, outcome, key decisions/learnings, member-ID list for drill-down.
- Summaries are DERIVED artifacts, regenerated from source items + history — never mutations of tracked items (no changelog drag, no history noise); stored like other derived indexes (semantic ledger precedent).
- Context assembly (pm-4k6b knowledge-density signal) and search can return a rollup as a cheap stand-in when the budget cannot afford members, with drill-down via existing get/history.
- Complements pm-o79o, which handles the DISK/git cost of old data; this handles the TOKEN/context cost. Together they define the aging story for million-item, decades-spanning workspaces.

Start with a spike + design note; the clustering fidelity question (mechanical rollup vs. model-assisted summaries via an extension hook) is explicitly part of the spike.
Workspace memory tiers: derived rollup summaries of closed-item epochs keep decades-old work queryable in bounded tokens

Design note settles derived-artifact placement, regeneration triggers, and invalidation (aligned with the semantic-ledger precedent); prototype generates epoch/epic rollup summaries on a pm-mi2x synthetic workspace; pm context and pm search can surface rollup records as compact stand-ins under a token budget with drill-down IDs to full items; source items and their history streams remain byte-untouched (derived-only guarantee proven by test); regeneration is deterministic for a fixed workspace state; small workspaces skip the tier entirely with zero configuration
